title: Global Governance in the Twenty-First Century: Sustain, Reform, Transform
gist: Carnegie's Miles Kahler argues that the US, China and Russia have become strongman-led, nationalist, revisionist powers that are dismantling rather than merely free-riding on the post-1945 order, yet globalization has proven resilient and finance largely untouched by the backlash. He predicts no new Bretton Woods or hegemonic replacement; instead the future order will be cobbled together by middle powers, regional institutions and private/NGO networks, risking either stagnation or fragmentation, with two competing variants of liberalism (EU 'micro-liberalism' vs Global-South 'macro-liberalism') contesting the outcome.

themes.theme: Strongman regimes as the driver of disorder

insights.claim: Three domestically driven features of major-power behavior matter more than polarity for global order: nationalist programs that kill transnational ideological appeal, strongman regimes that centralize foreign-policy authority, and camarillas of leader-loyalists who displace expert bureaucracies.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Frames geopolitical risk as a function of leader psychology and information bubbles rather than capability balances, implying higher tail-risk of miscalculation that markets underprice.
insights.quote: More important than whether the world is unipolar, bipolar, or multipolar are three domestically driven features of major power competition and conflict
insights.locator: Major Powers and Global Order: Nationalism and Strongman Regimes

insights.claim: The US, China, Russia, plus Turkiye and India have embraced backward-looking nationalist programs (MAGA, 'greater Russia', pre-humiliation China) that treat multilateral institutions as constraints on national power rather than amplifiers of it.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Signals durable, bipartisan/cross-regime hostility to global institutions, so expect continued erosion of trade and norms-based rules regardless of who governs.
insights.locator: Nationalist Programs

insights.claim: Camarillas of leader-dependent loyalists have powerful incentives to feed the leader only information that confirms his worldview, producing miscalculation; the 2022 Ukraine invasion, US-China trade escalation and the Iran war all reflect this information-bubble syndrome.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Decision-making concentrated in echo chambers raises the probability of surprise wars and policy shocks, a structural argument for pricing geopolitical optionality.
insights.quote: acts as the leader’s window to the world, leaving much dependent on how accurate a depiction of external reality its members choose to provide
insights.locator: Eliminating Constraints: The Rise of Camarillas

insights.claim: Xi Jinping's anti-corruption purges hit a two-decade high, with 889,000 party members subject to discipline in 2024, and have extended to the party elite and military leadership.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Deepening purges signal elite instability and centralization in China, a tail risk for policy continuity and for foreign firms relying on stable counterparties.
insights.locator: Strongman Regimes / footnote on 2024 party discipline

insights.claim: The post-1945 norms of political autonomy and territorial integrity are being openly breached: Russia invaded Ukraine (2014, 2022), China presses South China Sea claims despite legal rebukes, and Trump has mused about seizing the Panama Canal, taking Greenland, and attacked Iran twice without UN or allied backing.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: A weakening anti-conquest norm raises territorial-risk premia across contested borders and undercuts the legal predictability cross-border investors rely on.
insights.locator: Strongman Regimes (Erosion of post-1945 foundational norms)

insights.claim: The Iran war is described as the first war in over a century the US has waged without allies or UN authorization, cementing perceptions that the US will never again anchor a rules-based order.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Reinforces a structural de-rating of US reliability as a security guarantor, pushing allies toward hedging and diversification.
insights.quote: The Iran war, the first war in more than a century that the United States has waged without allies or UN authorization, has only underscored perceptions of its disdain for any collaboration
insights.locator: Future Imperfect

insights.claim: Despite repeated shocks (9/11, Iraq/Afghanistan wars, the GFC, Ukraine invasions, COVID), globalization has slowed and shifted composition rather than retreated, with intangibles—IP, services, and people (especially students)—now driving cross-border integration.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Bets on outright deglobalization are likely wrong; the tradable shift is toward intangibles and services, redirecting where growth and rents accrue.
insights.quote: The pace and characteristics of globalization have slowed, but large-scale retreat has not occurred.
insights.locator: The Global Economy in a Century of Shocks

insights.claim: Trump's tariff war has had more limited effects than 'liberation day' predictions because the US is a smaller share of global trade (especially manufacturing) than commonly believed and disruption is concentrated in US-China bilateral trade.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Tariff shock is more contained than feared; the real action is US-China bilateral decoupling plus trade rerouting, not a global collapse in flows.
insights.locator: The Global Economy in a Century of Shocks

insights.claim: Unlike the 1930s, the rest of the world has responded to US protectionism not by matching it but by accelerating new trade agreements (RCEP, AfCFTA, CPTPP, ACFTA 3.0).
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Trade liberalization is migrating around the US; exposure to these blocs (Asia, Africa, Pacific) is where rule-based market access is still expanding.
insights.locator: The Global Economy in a Century of Shocks

insights.claim: US tariffs on Chinese goods caused increased Chinese investment in Southeast Asia and rising exports from those economies, while US financial firms still lead Chinese listings on the Hong Kong exchange and Chinese firms quickly engineered workarounds to rare-earth export curbs.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Restrictions act like whack-a-mole; trade and capital reroute through third countries, so watch ASEAN transshipment and HK listings as the real flow channels.
insights.quote: Government efforts to restrict or prohibit profit-seeking in the global economy often resemble a game of whack-a-mole
insights.locator: Globalization and the Limits of Strongman Nationalism

themes.theme: Finance and monetary institutions spared the backlash

insights.claim: Nationalist hostility to global institutions is highly selective and has spared the monetary/financial core: the IMF, World Bank and BIS have not been central targets, Trump criticized but did not threaten withdrawal from the IFIs, and his administration has not moved against the Fed's BIS participation.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: The plumbing of global finance and central-bank coordination is more durable than headline geopolitics implies, supporting continuity in monetary cooperation.
insights.locator: Political and Cultural Backlash to Globalization (absence of backlash)

insights.claim: Elites who back nationalist strongmen quietly embrace financial globalization: the Trump administration promotes cybercurrencies (also a means of enrichment), Chinese elites set up Singapore family offices as insurance, and sanctioned Russian oligarchs find new settlement channels.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Elite self-interest underwrites the persistence of open finance and crypto rails even amid rhetorical closure—capital mobility is a structural constant.
insights.quote: In an era of rhetorical closure and open economy authoritarians, international finance seems immune to the vagaries of major power politics.
insights.locator: Political and Cultural Backlash to Globalization

insights.claim: New avenues of integration have enhanced evasion: cryptocurrency, offshore financial centers, and fleets of shadow tankers carrying sanctioned petroleum keep flows moving around restrictions.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Sanctions leakage via shadow fleets and crypto means commodity and capital flows are stickier than enforcement assumes—relevant to oil supply and sanctions trades.
insights.locator: Globalization and the Limits of Strongman Nationalism

insights.claim: Top-down restoration of order via major powers is unlikely: a new Bretton Woods is 'fanciful', Chinese hegemonic replacement of the US is improbable for reasons of identity and economic orientation, and a great-power concert founders because today's powers are revisionist, unlike the conservative Concert of Europe.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Don't expect a grand bargain to re-anchor the system; the realistic baseline is messy, bottom-up patchwork governance, not a reset.
insights.quote: A bargain that would produce a new Bretton Woods agreement for global economic governance seems fanciful
insights.locator: Rebuilding Global Governance from the Top Down

insights.claim: Ryan Hass reads the softer US tone toward China (Trump's G2 reference; the 2026 NDS dropping 'great power competition' for 'stable peace, fair trade, respectful relations') as an effort to 'buy time and build insulation', not a genuine strategic reorientation.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Treat any US-China detente as tactical and reversible; insulation-building implies continued decoupling under a calmer surface.
insights.locator: Rebuilding Global Governance from the Top Down (G2 / NDS)

insights.claim: Stable spheres of influence are unworkable because the powers that populate proximate spheres (ASEAN, the Middle East, the Western Hemisphere) are substantial economic/military powers that won't accept hierarchy without coercion, and an integrated world economy resists carving into exclusive blocs.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Bloc-formation theses are overstated; mid-sized states will hedge between the US and China rather than be captured, keeping trade multidirectional.
insights.locator: Rebuilding Global Governance from the Top Down (spheres of influence)

themes.theme: Middle powers and regional institutions as substitutes

insights.claim: Middle powers are hedging via trade diversification—Canada under Carney struck a China strategic partnership and accelerated Mercosur/India talks; Australia completed deals across Asia, the Gulf, UK and EU—but US-security-dependent allies still find it hard to break from Washington's hard line on China.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Watch security-dependence as the binding constraint: economic de-risking proceeds but allies stay tethered to US China policy via security leverage.
insights.locator: Middle Powers as System Sustainers

insights.claim: The Trump administration uses the implicit threat of withdrawing security commitments as tariff leverage, escalating during the Iran war with warnings that NATO faces a 'very bad future' over the Strait of Hormuz and announced withdrawal of 5,000 US troops from Germany.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Security guarantees are being explicitly transactionalized into trade bargaining, raising the cost and unpredictability of alliance reliance for Europe and Asia.
insights.locator: Middle Powers as System Sustainers

insights.claim: BRICS is incoherent and a disappointing reform vehicle: India and China have fought border skirmishes and India joined the Quad, the group could not respond to the 2026 Israel/US attacks on member Iran because of Iran's attacks on member UAE, and only in 2025 did China give up WTO developing-country status.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Don't overweight BRICS as a unified counter-bloc or alt-reserve architecture; internal rivalry makes coordinated action (and de-dollarization) unlikely.
insights.locator: Middle Powers as System Sustainers (the new influentials)

insights.claim: India's diplomatic capacity badly lags its great-power aspirations: it ranks 11th in diplomatic posts (below Turkiye) with 4,888 global staff versus China's 8,000 and the US's 30,000 (as of Dec 2023).
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: A capacity gap means rising-power economic weight will not translate quickly into governance influence—tempering expectations for India-led leadership.
insights.locator: Middle Powers as System Sustainers (diplomatic capacity)

insights.claim: The EU, an economy equal in scale to the US and China, is the strongest backer of the existing order (Ukraine support, ICC, trade activism via India and Mercosur deals), but internal division blocks a coherent response to Chinese import floods and far-right nationalist parties.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: The EU is the residual anchor of the liberal order but is internally constrained—its trade activism (India, Mercosur) is where rule-based access still expands.
insights.locator: Regional Institutions as Supplements and Substitutes

insights.claim: Multilateral forums can now produce agreements without major powers—nineteen countries signed the 2025 G20 declaration despite the US boycott, and COP30 reached a deal despite US absence—but these outcomes are low-cost and non-binding.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Governance-without-Washington is possible but toothless; expect symbolic accords, not enforceable commitments, where the US opts out.
insights.locator: Future Imperfect (G20 / COP 30)

insights.claim: Absentee major-power participation risks stagnation—legacy institutions survive but stop being centers of negotiation or action—while leaderless 'islands of cooperation' risk fragmentation if they fail to connect under an active global umbrella.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Base case is gridlock plus fragmented mini-deals, raising coordination costs and policy uncertainty across climate, health, and trade.
insights.quote: A leaderless convoy, disconnected from stagnant global institutions, could easily drift or run aground
insights.locator: Future Imperfect (Avoiding Stagnation and Fragmentation)

insights.claim: Plurilateral workarounds aligned to existing norms are emerging within the WTO umbrella: the Multi-Party Interim Appeal Arbitration Arrangement (MPIA) replaced the disabled Appellate Body, and the March 2026 ministerial produced a legally binding plurilateral e-commerce agreement despite being judged a failure.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Dispute resolution and digital-trade rules are quietly being rebuilt via opt-in clubs—where the actual, enforceable trade governance now lives.
insights.locator: Future Imperfect (plurilateral model)

insights.claim: The future contest is between EU/liberal-democracy 'micro-liberalism' (global standards, human rights, pooled sovereignty, individual freedom) and Global-South 'macro-liberalism' (self-determination, diverse national development paths, guarding against predatory great powers).
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Regulatory and standards battles (rights, ESG, harmonization vs sovereignty) will fracture along this axis, shaping which markets converge on which rulebooks.
insights.locator: Uncertainty and the Prospects for Global Order

insights.claim: The most likely outcome is not predatory closure but a resilient patchwork: a core of integrated collaborative democracies plus looser arrangements incorporating autocracies, though it is unclear such a system could surmount financial crises, pandemics or climate change.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Plan for a durable but uneven, multi-speed order rather than collapse or restoration—diversification across rule-systems beats betting on one bloc.
insights.quote: a patchwork of more integrated and collaborative democracies and looser arrangements that incorporate domestic regimes of different types, including autocracies
insights.locator: Uncertainty and the Prospects for Global Order

insights.claim: The new influentials, as major beneficiaries of the open economy, are unlikely to overturn the economic order even as the US retreats into 'illiberal and transactional behavior', but will resist outside pressure to harmonize their societies on rights or regulatory grounds.
insights.speaker: Miles Kahler
insights.so_what: Emerging powers will defend open trade while rejecting Western rights/regulatory conditionality—expect persistence of trade openness but fragmentation of standards.
insights.locator: Uncertainty and the Prospects for Global Order
